description: Replace synchronous requests.get pagination loop with async aiohttp. Each category page fetch becomes an awaitable coroutine. The pagination within a single category runs as a gather() burst — first page fetches numFound, then ALL remaining pages fire concurrently in a single asyncio.gather() call.

architecture_proposal.function_signature: async def crawl_category(session: aiohttp.ClientSession, api_key: str, category_url: str, category_count: int, ctx: RunContext, demo: bool = False, max_demo_pages: int = 3) -> list
architecture_proposal.protocol_insight: The Costco search.costco.com navigation API returns numFound on the first page response. This means page 1 MUST be sequential (to learn total_pages), but pages 2..N can ALL fire simultaneously. This is the critical optimization — fan-out after the probe request.
implementation: 1. Accept an aiohttp.ClientSession (connection pool shared across all categories)
2. Fetch page 1 with await session.get() — extract numFound, calculate total_pages
3. Build list of start offsets: range(24, numFound, 24), capped by max_demo_pages if demo
4. Fire ALL remaining pages via asyncio.gather(*[fetch_page(session, start) for start in offsets])
5. Flatten results into single docs list, return
architecture_proposal.why_this_pattern: Fan-out-after-probe eliminates the sequential page loop entirely. A 50-page category that took 50 serial requests now completes in the latency of ~1 request (all pages in flight simultaneously).
architecture_proposal.cleanup: Remove the synchronous requests import. Remove the for-loop pagination block (lines 83-118).

zombie_protection: aiohttp.ClientTimeout(total=20, connect=5) on the session. asyncio.gather(*tasks, return_exceptions=True) — any page that throws gets logged via ctx.event() and excluded from results, never blocks the batch.

description: Replace the sequential for-loop over crawl_targets (line 98) with asyncio.gather() across ALL categories simultaneously. This is the outer concurrency layer — every category crawl fires in parallel, and within each, pages fan out (COST-001). Result: full catalog extraction becomes a single massive concurrent burst.

architecture_proposal.function_signature: async def scrape_costco_catalog(ctx: RunContext, demo: bool = False, demo_url: str = '/jewelry.html') -> tuple[list, list, dict]
architecture_proposal.protocol_insight: The API key and megamenu fetch are lightweight one-shot calls that remain sequential (they gate the crawl). But once crawl_targets are resolved, ALL categories launch concurrently. The aiohttp.ClientSession is created once and shared via asyncio.gather().
implementation: 1. Keep Steps 1-3 (key, megamenu, parse) as sequential awaits — they are single requests
2. Create one aiohttp.ClientSession with TCPConnector(limit=0) for unlimited connections
3. Build coroutine list: [crawl_category(session, api_key, url, ...) for category in crawl_targets]
4. results = await asyncio.gather(*coros, return_exceptions=True)
5. Filter out exceptions (log them), flatten successful results into all_products
6. Dedupe and sanitize as before (these are CPU-bound, fast, no change needed)
architecture_proposal.why_this_pattern: TCPConnector(limit=0) means no artificial connection cap. All categories + all their pages resolve concurrently. With ~1274 categories averaging ~5 pages each, this is ~6000 requests flying simultaneously instead of sequentially.
architecture_proposal.cleanup: Remove 'import requests'. Remove sequential for-loop (lines 96-110). Add 'import asyncio, aiohttp'.

zombie_protection: Session-level ClientTimeout(total=30, sock_read=15). Any category that fully fails gets logged with ctx.event('category_crawl_failed') and excluded — never blocks gather(). A semaphore can be added later if needed, but baseline is uncapped.

description: Convert run_get_key() to async. Single request, minimal impact, but required so the caller can be fully async without threading hacks.

architecture_proposal.protocol_insight: Single GET to costco.com homepage, regex extract authentification_token. Trivial conversion — just swap requests.get for await session.get().
implementation: 1. Accept shared aiohttp.ClientSession
2. async with session.get('https://www.costco.com') as resp: text = await resp.text()
3. Regex extract and return api_key
architecture_proposal.why_this_pattern: Keeps the entire pipeline in one event loop. No sync-to-async bridging needed.

zombie_protection: ClientTimeout(total=10) on this specific call. If costco.com is down, fail fast with RuntimeError.

description: Convert run_get_megamenu() to async. Single API call to search.costco.com megamenu endpoint.

architecture_proposal.function_signature: async def run_get_megamenu(session: aiohttp.ClientSession, api_key: str) -> dict
architecture_proposal.protocol_insight: Same pattern as COST-003. Single GET, return JSON. Swap requests.get for await session.get(), use await resp.json().
implementation: 1. Accept shared session
2. await session.get(url, headers=headers, params=params)
3. return await resp.json()
architecture_proposal.why_this_pattern: Consistency. All HTTP I/O goes through one aiohttp session.

zombie_protection: Same session-level timeout. Megamenu is a single call — if it fails, the pipeline aborts immediately.

description: Convert run_pipeline() to async. The scrape stage becomes 'await scrape_costco_catalog()'. Category structuring and storage remain synchronous (CPU-bound, sub-50ms) — they run inline after the await returns. Entry point uses asyncio.run().

architecture_proposal.function_signature: async def run_pipeline(ctx: RunContext, demo: bool) -> None
architecture_proposal.protocol_insight: Only the scrape stage is async. Everything after it (build_category_tree, prune, persist_*) is pure CPU/SQLite — no benefit from async. The async boundary is clean: one await, then sync processing.
implementation: 1. async def run_pipeline(ctx, demo)
2. products_flat, parsed, metrics = await scrape_costco_catalog(ctx, demo)
3. Everything else (category tree, storage) stays synchronous — called inline
4. main() calls asyncio.run(run_pipeline(ctx, demo))
architecture_proposal.why_this_pattern: Minimal blast radius. Async only where it matters (network I/O). No unnecessary async infection of the storage layer.
architecture_proposal.cleanup: Add 'import asyncio'. Change main() to use asyncio.run().

zombie_protection: Top-level asyncio.run() with a global timeout wrapper if needed. ctx.span() still captures duration and exceptions — no observability changes required.

description: Add a POST /run-etl endpoint (or integrate with the future UI button). This endpoint calls the async pipeline directly within the FastAPI event loop — no subprocess, no thread. Returns the delta report when complete.

architecture_proposal.function_signature: @app.post('/run-etl') async def run_etl(demo: bool = False) -> dict
architecture_proposal.protocol_insight: FastAPI is already async (uvicorn event loop). Since the pipeline is now fully async, we can await it directly in an endpoint handler. The UI button hits this endpoint, gets back the report JSON.
implementation: 1. Import run_pipeline and RunContext
2. POST /run-etl creates RunContext, awaits run_pipeline(ctx, demo)
3. Returns ctx.report as JSON response
4. Future: add delta calculation (compare new products vs previous snapshot before DB rebuild)
architecture_proposal.why_this_pattern: Zero subprocess overhead. The async pipeline runs natively in FastAPI's event loop. Sub-second UI response for the trigger, streamed progress via SSE later if needed.

zombie_protection: Request timeout via middleware or asyncio.wait_for(). If the full crawl exceeds 60s, return partial results with error status.

description: Stop dropping all tables every run. Implement delta-aware storage: snapshot previous state before rebuild, then diff. This enables the arbitrage delta-report (price drops, out-of-stock, new items).

architecture_proposal.function_signature: def snapshot_previous_state(db_path) -> dict[str, dict]; def compute_delta(old_snapshot: dict, new_products: list) -> dict
architecture_proposal.protocol_insight: Before recreate_costco_db(), read all current products into a dict keyed by product ID. After new persist, diff: new IDs = new items, missing IDs = out-of-stock, price changes = arbitrage signals.
implementation: 1. snapshot_previous_state(): SELECT * FROM products → {id: {min_price, max_price, name, ...}}
2. Run normal pipeline (recreate + persist)
3. compute_delta(old, new): categorize into price_drops[], price_increases[], new_items[], removed_items[]
4. Return delta dict alongside the normal report
architecture_proposal.why_this_pattern: Minimal schema change. The snapshot-before-rebuild pattern gives us delta without adding versioning complexity. Phase 1 remains a full rebuild, but now we capture what changed.

zombie_protection: If previous DB doesn't exist or is corrupt, snapshot returns empty dict — delta shows everything as 'new'. No crash path.

description: Add aiohttp to dependencies. Remove requests (it will no longer be used after COST-001 through COST-004).

architecture_proposal.protocol_insight: aiohttp is the only new runtime dependency. Everything else (asyncio) is stdlib.
implementation: 1. Add 'aiohttp>=3.9,<4' to [project.dependencies]
2. Remove 'requests>=2.31.0' after all sync code is replaced
architecture_proposal.why_this_pattern: aiohttp is the standard async HTTP client for Python. No need for httpx — aiohttp has lower overhead and better connection pooling for high-concurrency bursts.
architecture_proposal.cleanup: pip install -e . to rebuild local env.
